coredata.dc:title: Green one-pot synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots as new fluorescent nanosensors for determination of salinomycin and maduramicin in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Magdy, Galal
Abdel Hakiem, Ahmed F.
Belal, Fathalla
Abdel-Megied, Ahmed M.
coredata.dc:description: A simple green hydrothermal method was proposed for synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) using citric acid and thiosemicarbazide. The produced N,S-CQDs were subjected to extensive spectroscopic characterization and applied as fluorescent nanosensors for the sensitive spectrofluorimetric determination of salinomycin and maduramicin directly without prior derivatization for the first time. The obtained N,S-CQDs showed strong emission band at 430 nm after excitation at 360 nm. The native fluorescence of N,S-CQDs was found to be quenched by the addition of increased concentrations of each drug. Method validation revealed a wide linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching of N,S-CQDs and the concentration of each drug in the range of 10.0–300.0 μM with detection limits of 2.07 μM and 1.34 μM for salinomycin and maduramicin, respectively. The developed method has been efficiently applied for estimation of analytes in six raw matrices with high recoveries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen sulfur-carbon quantum dots
Salinomycin
Maduramicin
Fluorescent nanosensors
Feed premixes
Animal tissues

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence response of N,S-CQDs to SAL or MAD
Optimization of factors affecting the fluorescence sensing of SAL and MAD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The typical HRTEM images of the N,S-CQDs, (B) FT-IR spectra of N,S-CQDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N,S-CQDs in aqueous solution upon addition of various concentrations of SAL (from top to bottom: 0 μM, 10.0 μM, 25.0 μM, 50.0 μM, 100.0 μM, 150.0 μM, 200.0 μM, 250.0 μM, 300.0 μM), (B). MAD (from top to bottom: 0 μM, 10.0 μM, 25.0 μM, 50.0 μM, 150.0 μM, 200.0 μM, 250.0 μM, 300.0 μM), (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of N,S-CQDs (a), N,S-CQDs with 300.0 μM of SAL (b) N,S-CQDs with 300.0 μM of MAD (c).

Fluorescence emission spectra of the N,S-CQDs under optimum conditions upon addition of various concentrations of SAL in feed premix (a), cow meat (b), chicken meat (c), chicken eggs (d), liver (e), and pasteurized milk (f) (from top to bottom: 0 µg/g, 2.5 µg/g, 5.0 µg/g, 10.0 µg/g, 20.0 µg/g).

Fluorescence emission spectra of the N,S-CQDs under optimum conditions upon addition of various concentrations of MAD in feed premix (a), cow meat (b), chicken meat (c), chicken eggs (d), liver (e), and pasteurized milk (f) (from top to bottom: 0 µg/g, 2.5 µg/g, 5.0 µg/g, 10.0 µg/g, 20.0 µg/g).
General procedure for hydrothermal synthesis and application of N,S-CQDs.
Analytical performance data for the proposed Methods.
Intraday and interday precision data for the determination of the studied drugs by the proposed method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Each result is the average of three separate determinations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Robustness evaluation of the proposed method.
Linearity equations for SAL and MAD in different matrices.
Recovery percentages of SAL and MAD in different matrices.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each result is the average of three separate determinations.

head.ce:title: Green one-pot synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots as new fluorescent nanosensors for determination of salinomycin and maduramicin in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Kafrelsheikh University, Kafrelsheikh, P.O. Box 33511, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Kafrelsheikh University, Kafrelsheikh, P.O. Box 33511, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura, P.O. Box 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura, P.O. Box 35516, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Pacific University Oregon, Hillsboro, OR 97123, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Pacific University Oregon, Hillsboro, OR, 97123, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple green hydrothermal method was proposed for synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) using citric acid and thiosemicarbazide. The produced N,S-CQDs were subjected to extensive spectroscopic characterization and applied as fluorescent nanosensors for the sensitive spectrofluorimetric determination of salinomycin and maduramicin directly without prior derivatization for the first time. The obtained N,S-CQDs showed strong emission band at 430 nm after excitation at 360 nm. The native fluorescence of N,S-CQDs was found to be quenched by the addition of increased concentrations of each drug. Method validation revealed a wide linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching of N,S-CQDs and the concentration of each drug in the range of 10.0–300.0 μM with detection limits of 2.07 μM and 1.34 μM for salinomycin and maduramicin, respectively. The developed method has been efficiently applied for estimation of analytes in six raw matrices with high recoveries.

ce:section.ce:para: Salinomycin (SAL) and maduramicin (MAD) belong to the class of polyether antibiotics. They are a group of natural ionophore substances that are produced by some strains of Streptomyces species. They are usually used in animal feeding for prevention and treatment of coccidiosis, a disease caused by Eimeria protozoa. In addition, they are used as growth promoters in pigs and cattles, by improving feeding efficiency and rate of weight gaining (Aiello & Moses, 2012). Ionophores were found to be toxic for highly susceptible species, such as equines. Maduramicin has also developed human toxicity represented in renal failure and polyneuropathy with rhabdomyolysis (Sharma, Bhalla, Varma, Jain, & Singh, 2005).

Chemically, the structure of SAL is sodium;(2R)-2-[(2R,5S,6R)-6-[(2S,3S,4S,6R)-6-[(3S,5S,7R,9S,10S,12R,15R)-3-[(2R,5R,6S)-5-ethyl-5-hydroxy-6-ethyloxan-2-yl]-15-hydroxy-3,10,12-trimethyl-4,6,8-trioxadispiro[4.1.57.35] pentadec −13-en-9-yl] −3-hydroxy-4-methyl-5-oxooctan-2-yl]-5-methyloxan-2-yl]butanoate (Supplementary material Fig. S1A), while that of MAD is azanium;2-[(2R,3S,4S,5R,6S)-6-[(1R)-1-[(2S,5R,7S,8R,9S)-2- [(2R,5S) −5- [(2R,3S,5R)-3- [(4S,5S,6S)−4,5- dimethoxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl] oxy-5-[(2S,3S,5R,6S)−6- hydroxy-3,5,6-trimethyloxan-2-yl]oxolan-2-yl]-5-methyloxolan-2-yl]-7-hydroxy −2,8- dimethyl-1,10dioxaspiro [4.5]decan-9-yl]ethyl]-2-hydroxy-4,5-dimethoxy-3-methyloxan-2-yl]acetate (Supplementary material Fig. S1B) (Rokka, Jestoi, & Peltonen, 2013).

The official maximum levels of polyether ionophores were established by the European Commission, where SAL and MAD are added to animal feed as sodium salts at levels of 20–70 mg/kg and 5.0 mg/kg, respectively (Jones, Solis, Hughes, Castaldo, & Toler, 1990; Rokka et al., 2013). Moreover, the official maximum residue limits (MRLs) in foodstuffs were established by European Commission in 2009. MRLs were adopted for 0.05 mg/kg and 0.7 mg/kg for MAD and SAL, respectively (Rokka et al., 2013). Because these drugs are administered to animals for a long period, their residues may be present in animal tissues and may be harmful to human health. Accordingly, developing analytical method for determination of such drugs in feed premixes and food samples is urgently needed to confirm satisfactory limits.

Many reports had been developed for HPLC determination of SAL and MAD with UV detection (Campbell & Nayeri, 2006; Tavčar-Kalcher, Pavšič-Vrtač, & Vengušt, 2009), fluorescence detection through derivatization with 1-(bromoacety1)pyrene (Asukabe et al., 1994), dansylhydrazine (Gliddon, Wright, Markantonatos, & Groth, 1988) or acetylation with acetic anhydride followed by esterificationwith 9-anthryIdiazomethane (Martinez & Shimoda, 1986), and also mass detection (Chang, Su, & Cheng, 2013; Pereira et al., 2016; Rokka et al., 2013). Salinomycin, MAD and other polyether antibiotics do not have absorbing chromophores, fluorescence or electrochemical activity. Therefore, common liquid chromatography detectors cannot be used for their direct determination. Consequently, they cannot be detected spectrophotometrically or spectrofluorimetrically without prior derivatization; which in turn makes the direct determination of such drugs is challenging.

Accordingly, the current work aimed at the development of a sensitive spectrofluorimetric method for determination of SAL and MAD in different raw matrices directly without prior derivatization for the first time. The developed method depends on the simple preparation of N,S-CQDs by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid as a carbon source and thiosemicarbazide as sulfur and nitrogen source in water as a green solvent, then the produced N,S-CQDs have been applied as a fluorescent nanosensor for determination of the studied drugs directly through quantitative quenching of N,S-CQDs native fluorescence.

In comparison to the previously reported methods, the current spectrofluorimetric method affords easy and cost-effective analytical solution instead of high-cost and sophisticated liquid chromatographic techniques such as HPLC or LC-MS that require precolumn or postcolumn derivatization. The proposed method is a direct method without any derivatization steps in contrast to the reported methods for SAL and MAD which required much more tedious and time-consuming steps.

The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials sized in the range of 2.0–10.0 nm. They have many advantages such as biocompatibility, low toxicity, good photoluminescence properties, easy synthesis, cheap starting materials, and chemical stability. Furthermore, the various functional groups such as carboxyl, carbonyl, epoxy and hydroxyl on the carbon core of the CQDs make them highly water soluble and suitable for easy functionalization using different species (Molaei, 2019, 2020). The CQDs have been used in many biosensors because they were found to be highly sensitive with detection limits (LOD) down to nanomolar (Chen, Gong, Cao, Zou, & Gu, 2018), picomolar (Yang, Liang, Luo, Liu, & Tang, 2013) or even femtomolar ranges (Liu et al., 2013).

Doped CQDs are usually prepared by doping of the hetero-atoms such as sulphur (S), nitrogen (N), boron (B), fluorine (F), and phosphorous (P) into the general structure of the CQDs (Li, Yang, & Xu, 2019). Generally, doping is mostly carried out to enhance the photoluminescence properties of CQDs (Atabaev, 2018). The doped CQDs still possess all the advantages of undoped CQDs. Moreover, doping CQDs with non-metallic elements can adjust their structures and compositions, and also possess many advantages over the heavy metal-containing semiconductor-based QDs (Wu, Jiang, Gu, & Tong, 2017). Therefore, many researchers have paid great attention to these species, particularly, those doped with sulfur and nitrogen atoms Chandra et al., 2013; Wu et al., 2017).

From literature survey, various methods were described for synthesis of fluorescent N,S-CQDs, such as hydrothermal methods using cysteine and citric acid as starting materials (Wu et al., 2017), hydrothermal treatment of cystamine dihydrochloride and citric acid (Chen, Liu, Li, Xu, & Qiao, 2017), microwave-assisted methods (Wang, Zhuang, & Ni, 2015), carbonizing organics methods (Sun et al., 2013) and chemical oxidation methods (Amjadi, Manzoori, Hallaj, & Azizi, 2017). The utilization of expensive instrumentation in most previous reports like microwave oven at very high temperature (Wang et al., 2015) or complicated chemical interaction under drastic conditions of boiling with concentrated sulfuric acid as in carbonizing organics (Sun et al., 2013) can limit the use of these methods and make them inadequate. Actually, these complicated preparatory conditions will surely cost much, consume time and accordingly, decrease method greenness. Thus, it would be a great challenge to develop a simple, green, cost effective, and high yield method for preparation of N,S-CQDs as performed in the current work. Moreover, the use of citric acid and thiosemicarbazide as starting materials for N,S-CQDs preparation without the need of expensive instrumentation or drastic conditions makes the current method more suitable and cost effective.

Salinomycin sodium and MAD were kindly provided by BIOVET Joint Stock Company (Peshtera-Bulgaria). Citric acid (CA), thiosemicarbazide (TS), acetic acid, sodium acetate, dipotassium hydrogen phosphate, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, boric acid, sodium hydroxide and methanol were purchased of Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). The membrane filters of 0.45 µm pore size (Phenomenex, USA) were utilized. Double distilled water was utilized throughout the experiments. All chemicals and reagents are of analytical grade. Cow meat, chicken meat, liver samples, chicken eggs and pasteurized milk were purchased from the local market. The feed premixes were provided by a commercial veterinary retailer.

Stock solutions (1.0 mM) of SAL and MAD were prepared in methanol and different concentrations (10.0–300.0 μM) were obtained by diluting the stock solutions. N,S-CQDs solutions (1.0 g.L−1) were prepared in ultrapure water.

Fluorescence measurements were carried out using Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer equipped with Xenon flash lamp from Agilent Technologies (Santa Clara, CA 95051, United States). The instrument was operated at high voltage mode (800 V) at 5 nm slit width. The UV–Vis spectrophotometric measurements were carried out by UV-1601 PC Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The FT-IR spectra were detected by ThermoFisher Scientific Nicolet - iS10 FT-IR Spectrometer from Thermo Fisher Scientific (168 Third Avenue Waltham, MA, USA). It was equipped with a Ge/KBr beam splitter and a DTGS detector from 4000 to 1000 cm−1. All measurements were recorded as 32 scans at 4 cm−1 resolution. The morphology of the synthesized N,S-CQDs was investigated using JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) operated at 200 kV(JEOL, Tokyo). Ultrasonic bath, model SS 101H 230, (USA). Vortex mixer, Model IVM-300p (Gemmy industrial Corp, Taiwan), centrifuge, model 2-16P, (Germany), Hanna pH-meter (Romania) and Tissue Master-125 with 7-mm stainless steel generator probe (Omni International, GA, USA).

The N,S-CQDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment of citric acid as a source for carbon and thiosemicarbazide as a source for sulfur and nitrogen. Concisely, for preparation of N,S-CQDs, 0.68 g thiosemicarbazide (7.50 mmol) and 0.52 g citric acid (2.50 mmol) were mixed, afterwards, 20.0 mL of double distilled water was added followed by ultrasonication. The mixture was refluxed for 8 h at 110C° till formation of highly fluorescent N,S-CQDs, then it was left to cool and stored at room temperature for further uses.

For optimization of factors affecting the fluorescence sensing of the studied drugs, 100.0 μL volume of N,S-CQDs (1.0 g·L−1) was added to 100.0 μM of SAL or MAD solution. Afterwards, the emission fluorescence intensities were determined at 430 nm using an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. At optimum conditions, serial concentrations of each drug were added to 100.0 μL of N,S-CQDs (1.0 g·L−1) and the resultant fluorescence quenching spectra were measured at room temperature.

The samples of homogenous ground feed premix, cow meat, chicken meat, and liver (5 g each) were accurately weighed and spiked with aliquots of the studied drugs. The spiked samples were mixed with 10 mL of methanol as an extracting solvent and homogenized at 4000 rpm for 10 min to obtain uniform homogenate of each sample. Afterwards, they were vortexed for 2 min at room temperature, sonicated in an ultrasonic bath for 15 min and then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatants of all samples were filtered using 0.45-μm syringe filters. Then, the filtrate was transferred to 50.0 mL volumetric flasks and diluted to the mark with the same solvent. Different Known aliquots were transferred into 10.0 mL volumetric flasks and completed to the volume with double distilled water to obtain final concentrations in the range of 2.5–20.0 μg/gm for the studied drugs in all samples and then were measured as mentioned before. In case of organically produced eggs and pasteurized milk portions of 2.0 g and 2.0 mL, respectively were spiked and then were treated as mentioned above (Belal, Abd El-Razeq, Fouad, Zayed, & Fouad, 2018).

Spectrofluorimetric determination of SAL and MAD was selected in the proposed study as it affords simple, rapid, sensitive and economic method, especially; it wasn’t applied before for their estimation. In this work, a green, eco-friendly and simple preparation of N,S-CQDs was performed by a hydrothermal method using citric acid and thiosemicarbazide as starting materials. Then, a fluorescence probe for sensitive determination of SAL and MAD was constructed depending on the quantitative quenching of the native fluorescence of N,S-CQDs by the studied drugs. The novelty of this work is addressed in being the first spectrofluorimetric method for determination of the cited drugs directly without prior derivatization in addition to the green and simple synthesis of N,S-CQDs using available starting materials and simple procedures.

In this study, green simple synthetic method was proposed for preparation of highly fluorescent N,S-CQDs. The proposed method depends on thermal treatment of citric acid as a carbon source and thiosemicarbazide as sulfur and nitrogen source in water as a green solvent as presented in Scheme 1 . The N,S-CQDs solution showed bright blue fluorescence under UV light and a long-standing homogeneous phase with no noticeable precipitation at room temperature for about two weeks. Characterization of N,S-CQDs was performed using UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM).

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of thiosemicarbazide, citric acid and N,S-CQDs are presented in Supplementary material
Fig. S2
A. As observed, the N,S-CQDs exhibited an obvious UV–Vis absorption peak with λmax at 330 nm, which is due to the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface states which produces strong emission (Anilkumar et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2010). In addition, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N,S-CQDs in aqueous solution are presented in Supplementary material
Fig. S2
B, upon exciting N,S-CQDs at 360 nm, they exhibited strong emission at 430 nm. The fluorescence spectra of N,S-CQDs shift by changing the excitation wavelength in the range of 340 to 370 nm and the strong fluorescence emission is observed after excitation at 360 nm, as illustrated in Supplementary material
Fig. S2
C. The native fluorescence of N,S-CQDs is attributed to presence of different function groups including OH, NH, CO, CC, CS and CN groups as illustrated in FT-IR spectra (Tang et al., 2019).

The HRTEM was utilized to study the surface morphology and determine the size of N,S-CQDs. The sample was loaded on carbon coated Cu-grid (200 mesh), and examined at 200 KV. The typical HRTEM images have revealed that the developed N,S-CQDs are well separated from each other with spherical shapes and their size is distributed in the range of 8.0 to 20.0 nm as presented in Fig. 1 A.

FT-IR analysis was also performed to examine the key functional groups on the surface of N,S-CQDs (Hu et al., 2017; Xue et al., 2016) (Fig. 1B). As seen in Fig. 1B, many characteristic vibrational modes as the broad band in the wavenumber range of 3500–3100 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching modes of NH/OH groups, and the stretching peak at 2065 cm−1 is due to CN vibration. The carbonyl moiety of carboxylic acid group is introduced at 1700 cm−1. The stretching peaks at 1621 cm−1 and 1233 cm−1 are attributed to CC and CS, respectively. A bending vibration band has exhibited at 578 cm−1 for the CH bond.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence response of N,S-CQDs to SAL or MAD
ce:section.ce:para: As presented in Scheme 1, the emitted blue fluorescence of the N,S-CDs solution was significantly quenched by the addition of the studied drugs. This could be as a result of destroying the surface passivation layer of the N,S-CQDs, which may be due to presence of large number of hydroxyl moieties in SAL and MAD (Feng, Ashley, Zhou, & Sun, 2018; Tashkhourian, Absalan, Jafari, & Zare, 2016; Xue et al., 2015). Fig. 2 A and B show the emission spectra of N,S-CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of SAL and MAD, respectively. As predicted, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs has decreased by increasing SAL or MAD concentrations.

In general, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms could be classified into static and dynamic quenching. Dynamic quenching is based on diffusion, quenching constants of the formed complexes increase with increasing temperature, and the maximum scatter collision quenching constant by all types of quenchers or the limiting diffusion constant is 2.0 × 1010 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1. In contrast, in static quenching, the quenching rate constants will decrease with the increase in temperature. The possible quenching mechanism can be presented by the Stern-Volmer equation, Equation (1) (Xiang, Tong, & Lin, 2007).
(1)
F 0 / F = 1 + K q τ 0
Q
= 1 + K s v
Q
where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of quencher, respectively; Kq represents the quenching rate constant; Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, [Q] is the quencher concentration, τ0 is the average lifetime (10−8 s) (Xiang et al., 2007).

The quenching experiments were carried out at 298 K. Applying of the quenching results to the previous equation resulted in Kq values of 4.60 × 1012 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1 and 7.80 × 1012 L·mol−1 s−1 for SAL and MAD, respectively, which were far larger than the maximum diffusion rate constant (2.0 × 1010 L·mol−1⋅s−1). Therefore, the quenching mechanism is concluded to be static rather than dynamic (Lakowicz, 2013). In addition, the emission spectra demonstrate that, the fluorescence intensity at 430 nm has declined about 40.20% and 34.02% in the presence of 300.0 μM of SAL and MAD, respectively. At the same time, change in the characteristic UV–Vis absorption of N,S-CQDs was investigated in presence of studied drugs (Fig. 2C), confirming that the quenching of N,S-CQDs is static in nature (Xue et al., 2015).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of factors affecting the fluorescence sensing of SAL and MAD

ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that, the pH of the solution not only affects the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs, but also plays a critical role in the interactions between the N,S-CQDs and target species (Zhang, Zhang, Guo, & Wang, 2008). The effect of pH on the quenching of fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs by either SAL or MAD was optimized in the range of pH 3–11 using different buffers (acetate, phosphate or borate buffer). It was found that, no significant change in the fluorescence intensities in different pH values with both drugs. Consequently, no adjustment of pH was needed in further studies and all the experiments were carried out in neutral conditions; which in turn adds an additional advantage to the proposed method.

The incubation time was investigated because of its great impact on fluorescence intensity. It was studied after the addition of the investigated drugs to N,S-CQDs. Fluorescence spectra were recorded at different time intervals from 1 min to 20 min. The reaction between the studied drugs and N,S-CQDs was found to be rapid and completed within about 1 min and the fluorescence signals were stable for more than 30 min; which adds another advantage to this method.
The developed method was validated according to ICHQ2(R1) recommendations (Guideline, 2005).

The linearity and range of the proposed method were assessed by plotting concentration in μM of each drug versus relative quenched fluorescence intensity. The linear range for both drugs was found to be 10.0 to 300.0 μM. The linear regression could be represented by the following equations, Eqs. ((2) and (3)):
(2)
F
0
- F
/
F
0
= 0 . 0019
C + 0 . 0853
R
2
= 0.9998
f o r
S A L
(3)
F
0
- F
/
F
0
= 0 . 0015
C + 0 . 0939
R
2
= 0.9997
f o r
M A D
where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs in the presence and absence of studied drugs, respectively, and C is the concentration of each drug in μM. Values reaching unity of correlation coefficient indicate acceptable linearity.
The obtained values of LOD and LOQ have proven that, the developed method has sufficient sensitivity for determining the analytes in the selected raw samples, as presented in Table 1 .

The precision of the method was carried out to investigate intra-day and inter-day precisions. They were calculated using three different concentrations and three replicates of each concentration. The studied drugs exhibited acceptable % RSD values of less than 2.0% (Table 2 ).

The robustness as an important full system validation parameter was tested. It represents the influence of minor changes in factors influencing the fluorescence sensing of the drugs. The effect of volume added of N,S,-CQDs (100.0 μL ± 1) and incubation time (1 min ± 30 sec.) have been studied with no significant effects on recovery percentages and % RSD values, as presented in Table 3 .

The specificity of the developed method was established by its ability to estimate the studied drugs in different matrices. Blank samples were prepared and analyzed under the optimum conditions. No interference was investigated in the produced fluorescence intensity, indicating the high specificity of the present study. In addition, excellent percentage recoveries at different studied matrices indicate that the developed method is highly specific.

The matrix effect is defined as a direct or indirect interference or alteration in response due to the presence of interfering or unintended substances in the sample (FDA, 2018). Relative matrix effect was assessed in this method by construction of calibration curves at different matrices. This procedure was reported to study the matrix effect of bioanalytical methods (Matuszewski, 2006; Pereira et al., 2016). The slopes of the calibration curves were calculated and insignificant differences were found between them (Table 4 ) (adopting the criteria of a relative standard deviation value of the slopes lower than 15%), demonstrating that unnoticeable endogenous substances present in the different matrices had no effect on the response of the detector (FDA, 2018; Matuszewski, 2006; Pereira et al., 2016). Since the studied matrices acted in the same way (the relative standard deviation values of the slopes for the six target matrices ranged from 0.326% to 1.9%), a relative matrix effect was not featured. Consequently, the method demonstrated to be selective and applicable with respect to different studied matrices.

The applicability of the developed method was assessed by analysis the studied drugs in spiked feed premixes, cow meat, chicken meat, chicken eggs, liver samples and pasteurized milk. Under the previously described experimental conditions, the studied drugs have shown linear calibration curves (spiked concentration in μg/gm vs. fluorescence quenching) in range of 2.5–20.0 μg/gm as spiked samples in the whole studied matrices with acceptable correlation coefficients and recovery percentages, as shown in Tables 4 and 5 . Figs. 3 and 4
represent the fluorescence emission spectra of the N,S-CQDs upon addition of various concentrations (2.5 µg/g, 5.0 µg/g, 10.0 µg/g, 20.0 µg/g) of SAL and MAD, respectively in feed premix (a), cow meat (b), chicken meat (c), chicken eggs (d), liver (e), and pasteurized milk (f). A significant fluorescence quenching was obtained with increasing concentrations of SAL or MAD.

This method proved to be suitable for estimation of both drugs in the studied matrices. Due to the high sensitivity down to 2.07 and 1.34 μM which are equivalent to 1.6 mg/kg and 1.25 mg/kg for SAL and MAD, respectively, the method could determine the studied drugs in animal feed samples below the stated limits where SAL and MAD are added to animal feed as sodium salts at levels of 20–70 mg/kg and 5.0 mg/kg, respectively (Jones et al., 1990; Rokka et al., 2013). Although the sensitivity of the proposed method isn't below the MRLs in foodstuffs (0.05 mg/kg and 0.7 mg/kg for MAD and SAL, respectively) (Rokka et al., 2013), the ability of the method to differentiate the studied drugs from matrix interferences and the adequate recoveries for each drug in each sample (Table 5) makes it suitable for the analysis of both drugs in foodstuffs mainly in case of their inappropriate use. It is well known that the risk of the veterinary drugs to accumulate in animal tissues above the permitted MRL range increases upon their incorrect use (Clarke et al., 2014).

To evaluate the precision of the method, determinations were performed for a set of three measurements under optimal conditions. The relative standard deviations for three determinations were lower than 2.0% in all spiked samples and high recoveries were obtained in the range of 97.14–101.87 % as presented in Table 5, indicating good precision of the method (Guideline, 2005).

The water soluble N,S–CQDs were prepared by a green simple one-pot hydrothermal method using citric acid as carbon source and thiosemcarbazide as nitrogen and sulfur source. The characterization of the produced N,S-CQDs was performed using different techniques. The developed N.S-CQDs have shown high stability and strong blue fluorescence intensities. The resulting obvious fluorescence quenching of N,S-CQDs with the increased concentrations of SAL or MAD, could lead to future promising fluorescence nanosensors for the studied drugs. The developed method was applied for determination of SAL and MAD in raw materials and extended to their determination in feed premix, cow meat, chicken meat, chicken eggs, liver samples and pasteurized milk with high recoveries. This method is the first spectrofluorimetric method for direct determination of SAL or MAD without prior derivatization. N,S-CQDs as a fluorescent probe for studied drugs has afforded many advantages as it represents a green method, very simple, rapid (less than 2 min) and economic. Additionally, it offers low detection limits and wide linearity ranges.
This article does not include any studies with human participants or animals carried out by any of the authors.
Informed consent is not applicable in this study.

Galal Magdy: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Ahmed F. Abdel Hakiem: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Fathalla Belal: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Resources, Supervision. Ahmed M. Abdel-Megied: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.128539.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
